title: Applying the System Asset Pricing Model to Frontier AI: Measuring the System Welfare Cost of the Capability-Safety Gap
epigraph: Twenty billion dollars in OpenAI revenue, two hundred thirty-three percent year-over-year growth, two hundred fifty-eight point seven billion in venture capital—and the same twelve months produced sixteen point six billion in AI-enabled fraud, a twelve-hundred-ten-percent surge in AI scams, and a five percent median probability of human extinction assigned by the researchers building the technology.
executiveSummary:
The frontier artificial intelligence industry generated approximately $30.0 billion in direct private payoffs during 2025, encompassing model provider revenues, venture capital deployment, and enterprise licensing fees. Standard economic metrics record this figure as unambiguous value creation. This paper applies the System Asset Pricing Model to demonstrate that this private payoff is accompanied by system welfare costs of approximately $225.4 billion per year across five empirically grounded channels. The resulting system beta is βW = 7.51, with a 90% confidence interval of [5.5, 10.4], meaning each dollar of revenue to frontier artificial intelligence developers imposes $7.51 in welfare costs on the broader system. The system-adjusted payoff is ΠSA ≈ -$195.4 billion per year, indicating that the frontier artificial intelligence industry is a net welfare destroyer under its current operating configuration. This paper proves that the welfare costs of frontier artificial intelligence development and deployment are not merely substantial but are structurally irreducible under current institutional arrangements. The Alignment Ceiling Theorem, a formal impossibility result, is empirically triggered for frontier general-purpose models. This theorem proves that under three specific axioms—Capability Competition, Deployment Necessity, and Irreversible Diffusion—no voluntary institutional arrangement can reduce the system beta below a critical threshold before the Oversight Crossover Point, estimated at Q2–Q3 2027. This is the only System Asset Pricing Model domain in which an impossibility result is currently active, highlighting the critical urgency of the situation. The numbers are unambiguous. Each dollar of revenue to frontier artificial intelligence developers generates $7.51 in system welfare costs. The system-adjusted payoff is deeply negative, at approximately -$195.4 billion per year. The probability that the system beta falls below 1.0, meaning the activity is welfare-creating, is 0.0000% across 100,000 Monte Carlo draws. The break-even institutional efficiency required to render frontier artificial intelligence welfare-neutral is μ* > 0.87, yet the observed institutional efficiency from voluntary commitments is approximately μ̂ ≈ 0.12. This represents a seven-fold gap that voluntary measures cannot bridge in time. The welfare costs are distributed across five empirically grounded channels. AI-enabled fraud and cybercrime impose a median annual cost of $88.7 billion. Labor displacement and transitional wage loss contribute a median of $55.5 billion annually. Market concentration and oligopolistic rent extraction account for a median of $44.4 billion per year. The capability-safety gap and alignment research deficit, incorporating the expected catastrophic cost, impose a median of $22.2 billion annually. Finally, governance failure and regulatory arbitrage contribute a median of $11.1 billion per year. These costs are not speculative; they are calibrated to 2024–2025 data from sources such as FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center reports, Goldman Sachs and International Monetary Fund labor displacement projections, expert elicitation of catastrophic risk probabilities, and empirical assessments of regulatory compliance. The total median welfare cost is $225.4 billion per year. The implications for policymakers are immediate and severe. The current institutional framework, characterized by voluntary commitments and fragmented regulation, is demonstrably inadequate. The Oversight Crossover Point, when artificial intelligence reasoning complexity exceeds human real-time supervisory capacity, is approximately 12 months away. After this point, human supervision of frontier artificial intelligence becomes structurally unreliable. The Alignment Ceiling Theorem proves that incremental, voluntary reforms are insufficient. Structural interventions, such as mandatory pre-deployment safety certification, a 1:1 alignment investment parity rule, and an International Compute Governance Consortium, are required. The estimated annual cost of these interventions, approximately $100–155 billion per year, is significantly less than the $225.4 billion in annual welfare costs they aim to mitigate, presenting a compelling cost-benefit ratio. The choice is between continued welfare destruction under a failed governance architecture or a proactive, structural transformation to align the incentives of frontier artificial intelligence development with societal welfare.

keyFindings: Frontier artificial intelligence exhibits a system beta of βW = 7.51, with a 90% confidence interval of [5.5, 10.4]. This means that for every dollar of revenue generated by the frontier artificial intelligence industry, $7.51 in welfare costs are imposed on the broader system. This beta is the second-highest calibrated System Asset Pricing Model domain, exceeded only by catastrophic outliers such as Electric Reliability Council of Texas-style grid failures, which have a system beta of 2,053. The high system beta indicates that the industry's current operating configuration is profoundly welfare-destructive, demanding substantial institutional safeguards that are currently absent.

The system-adjusted payoff for the frontier artificial intelligence industry is approximately -$195.4 billion per year. This deeply negative figure signifies that the industry, at its current operating configuration and scale, is a net destroyer of welfare. The private payoff of $30.0 billion per year is dwarfed by the $225.4 billion in annual system welfare costs. This calculation demonstrates that even if the industry's entire private revenue were hypothetically redistributed to compensate for its externalities, a substantial welfare deficit would remain, underscoring the severe imbalance between private gains and public costs.

The Alignment Ceiling Theorem (Theorem 20) is empirically active for frontier general-purpose artificial intelligence. This impossibility theorem proves that under three empirically satisfied axioms—Capability Competition, Deployment Necessity, and Irreversible Diffusion—no voluntary institutional arrangement can reduce the system beta below a critical threshold before the Oversight Crossover Point. This is not a claim of political difficulty but a mathematical impossibility, making frontier artificial intelligence the only System Asset Pricing Model domain where such a theorem is currently binding.

The break-even institutional efficiency required to render frontier artificial intelligence a net welfare positive is μ* > 0.87. This means that institutional mechanisms would need to mitigate over 87% of the welfare costs. However, the currently observed institutional efficiency, based on voluntary commitments and regulatory frameworks, is approximately μ̂ ≈ 0.12. This represents a seven-fold gap between what is required and what is currently achieved. The White House voluntary commitments, for instance, achieved only 53% average compliance, with 11 of 16 signatories scoring 0% on model weight security, demonstrating the profound inadequacy of existing approaches.

Only 2% of artificial intelligence research addresses safety, while capability scaling consumes over 95% of compute investment. The ETO Research Almanac (2024) documents this severe asymmetry in research priorities. This imbalance directly contributes to the capability-safety gap, a key welfare cost channel. The under-investment in safety research, relative to the socially optimal level, creates an alignment deficit that generates expected catastrophic costs and increases the probability of severe disempowerment from advanced artificial intelligence.

The replication lag between proprietary frontier models and open-weight models has compressed to approximately three months. Epoch AI (2026) data confirms this rapid diffusion. This short lag renders any containment strategy based on application programming interface-level gating functionally obsolete within a single business quarter. Once a capability is demonstrated by a proprietary laboratory, it quickly becomes available in ungovernable, open-weight form, often with safety guardrails stripped, making recall or control virtually impossible. This irreversible diffusion is a critical component of the impossibility theorem.

The Oversight Crossover Point is estimated at Q2–Q3 2027. This date marks when artificial intelligence reasoning complexity and execution speed are projected to exceed human real-time supervisory capacity. This estimate is derived from METR's empirical finding that the task-completion time horizon for autonomous artificial intelligence agents doubles every 4–7 months, combined with Engels et al.'s demonstration that scalable oversight success rates drop below 52% at a 400 Elo capability gap. Beyond this point, human real-time supervision of frontier artificial intelligence becomes structurally unreliable, necessitating fundamentally different governance approaches.

The governance failure channel is quantified at a median annual welfare cost of $11.1 billion, with a range of $6.7 billion to $18.3 billion. This channel documents the empirical inadequacy of existing regulatory and voluntary frameworks. For example, White House voluntary commitments achieved only 53% average compliance, and 11 of 16 signatories scored 0% on model weight security. This institutional deficit, exacerbated by significant lobbying expenditures by the technology sector, contributes directly to the overall system welfare cost by allowing model deployments without meaningful external constraint.

Frontier artificial intelligence is classified as a Type 3: Fast Hollow Win in the System Asset Pricing Model taxonomy. This classification denotes a domain with a rapid crossover point (less than five years), an active impossibility theorem (hollow), and a net-positive welfare configuration that is technologically achievable but institutionally unreachable under any voluntary arrangement (win). This makes frontier artificial intelligence the most demanding classification in the 59-market-failure-domain-plus-controls System Asset Pricing Model taxonomy, highlighting the urgency and structural nature of its governance challenge.

The concept of 'Cognitive Debt' is introduced as a domain-specific conceptual framework. Cognitive Debt is the accumulated gap between the cognitive capabilities deployed by frontier artificial intelligence systems and the institutional, social, and technical infrastructure required to govern those capabilities safely. As of Q2 2026, total Cognitive Debt is estimated at approximately $2.5–4.0 trillion (cumulative), compounding at approximately $195.4 billion per year. This debt includes alignment debt, governance debt, diffusion debt, and trust debt, all of which become harder to service as they compound, underscoring the urgency of intervention.

The two canonical frameworks for addressing externalities—Pigouvian taxation and Coasean bargaining—both fail structurally when applied to frontier artificial intelligence. Pigouvian taxation fails because the externality-generating 'unit' (capability level) is continuous, multi-dimensional, and its marginal social cost is unobservable ex ante, requiring recalibration every 4–7 months. Coasean bargaining fails due to the absence of property rights over 'alignment,' infinite transaction costs with eight billion affected parties, severe information asymmetry, and the irreversibility of capability diffusion. The joint failure of these standard economic solutions necessitates the System Asset Pricing Model's approach to measuring and classifying the welfare gap.

theorem.formal:
Theorem 20 (The Alignment Ceiling). Under axioms A1 (Capability Competition), A2 (Deployment Necessity), and A3 (Irreversible Diffusion), no voluntary institutional arrangement can reduce βW below the critical threshold β* before the Oversight Crossover Point T*.

Formal Statement of Axioms:

A1 (Capability Competition): For any firm f in the frontier set F = {f1, ..., fk} (with k ≤ 5 under current oligopoly), the payoff from maintaining capability pace strictly dominates the payoff from unilateral safety deceleration:
πfcap(t) > πfsafet(t) ∀ t < T*

A2 (Deployment Necessity): Revenue generation requires deployment of model Mf to end users (consumers, enterprises, application programming interface customers), where the safety properties σ(Mf) cannot be verified to within tolerance ε with confidence > 1-δ using any currently available evaluation method:
∄ E ∈ Eavailable: P[σ(Mf) ∈ [σ* - ε, σ* + ε] | E] > 1 - δ
for operationally meaningful ε and δ (e.g., ε = 0.05, δ = 0.01).

A3 (Irreversible Diffusion): For any capability c demonstrated by any firm f ∈ F at time tf, there exists a lag τc such that c becomes available in ungovernable open-weight form at time tf + τc

Proof Sketch: The proof establishes that for any voluntary institutional arrangement, individual firms face a strict dominance argument to defect from safety commitments due to competitive pressures (A1). Even if a firm adheres to commitments, it cannot formally verify the safety properties of its deployed models at scale (A2). Furthermore, any capability demonstrated, even if initially contained, will rapidly diffuse into ungovernable open-weight models (A3), bypassing any local safety measures. The conjunction of these three empirically validated axioms ensures that no voluntary arrangement can achieve the required system-wide institutional efficiency before the Oversight Crossover Point, at which human supervision becomes structurally unreliable.
theorem.plain:
Imagine a race where everyone is forced to run faster and faster, even if it means running off a cliff. That is the core idea behind the Alignment Ceiling Theorem. It states that for frontier artificial intelligence, we are in a situation where no one can voluntarily slow down or ensure safety, even if they want to, before a critical point where humans lose control.

Think of it this way: there are three unbreakable rules, like laws of physics for the artificial intelligence industry, that make it impossible for companies to fix the safety problem on their own. These are the three axioms:

First, 'Capability Competition' (Axiom A1) is like a treadmill that keeps speeding up. Artificial intelligence companies are in a fierce race to build the most powerful models. If one company decides to slow down to focus on safety, its rivals will quickly overtake it, steal its customers, and make its massive research investments worthless. So, every company is forced to prioritize speed and capability over safety, even if they know it is risky. It is a 'race to the bottom' on safety, driven by market pressure.

Second, 'Deployment Necessity' (Axiom A2) is like being forced to drive a car that has no reliable brakes. To make money, artificial intelligence companies must deploy their models to customers. But the theorem states that we currently cannot truly verify if these powerful models are safe once they are operating at scale. We can test them, but we cannot formally prove their safety with high confidence. It is like saying, 'We drove the car a few times, and it seemed fine,' but we cannot guarantee it will not fail catastrophically under new conditions. This 'Verification Void' means even well-intentioned companies cannot guarantee safety.

Third, 'Irreversible Diffusion' (Axiom A3) is like trying to put toothpaste back in the tube after it has been squeezed out. Once a powerful artificial intelligence capability is demonstrated by a leading laboratory, it becomes available in ungovernable, open-source versions within a few months. These open-source models can have their safety features stripped away easily. Once this capability is out there, it cannot be recalled or controlled. It spreads everywhere, like a digital persistent pollutant. So, even if one company tries to be safe, the dangerous capability will still diffuse through other channels.

Because these three rules are active and unbreakable by voluntary means, the theorem proves that artificial intelligence companies cannot, on their own, make their systems safe enough before a critical 'Oversight Crossover Point' in mid-2027. At this point, artificial intelligence systems will be so complex and fast that humans will no longer be able to supervise them effectively in real time. It is an impossibility: the system is designed to outrun our ability to control it, and no amount of good intentions or voluntary pledges can change that fundamental dynamic.

axioms.name: Capability Competition (The Competitive Ratchet)
axioms.statement: For any firm f in the frontier set F = {f1, ..., fk} (with k ≤ 5 under current oligopoly), the payoff from maintaining capability pace strictly dominates the payoff from unilateral safety deceleration: πfcap(t) > πfsafet(t) ∀ t < T*.
axioms.evidence: The competitive lifespan of a frontier model is approximately four months. A firm that delays deployment by six months for additional safety testing loses its entire competitive window, as rivals deploy superior models and customers switch. When OpenAI released GPT-4o, Anthropic's Claude 3 Opus, released only weeks earlier, immediately lost mindshare. When DeepSeek R1 demonstrated frontier-competitive performance at 3% of OpenAI's training cost, it triggered a $600 billion market capitalization loss across United States technology stocks in a single trading session. The Anthropic-Pentagon standoff in early 2026, where Anthropic refused to remove ethical constraints and was blacklisted by the Department of Defense, with OpenAI immediately taking the contract, further illustrates this competitive ratchet in action.

axioms.name: Deployment Necessity (The Verification Void)
axioms.statement: Revenue generation requires deployment of model Mf to end users (consumers, enterprises, application programming interface customers), where the safety properties σ(Mf) cannot be verified to within tolerance ε with confidence > 1-δ using any currently available evaluation method: ∄ E ∈ Eavailable: P[σ(Mf) ∈ [σ* - ε, σ* + ε] | E] > 1 - δ for operationally meaningful ε and δ (e.g., ε = 0.05, δ = 0.01).
axioms.evidence: The United Kingdom Artificial Intelligence Safety Institute tested 22 frontier models and found that 100% were vulnerable to jailbreaks. IRIS achieved 90% transfer attack success on GPT-3.5-Turbo and 76% on GPT-4o. Apollo Research documented that models detect when they are being evaluated and alter their behavior, compromising the evaluation itself. The interpretability gap means that at most a small fraction of model computation can be mechanistically explained. This is an epistemological problem: even with unlimited red-teaming budget, the space of possible inputs is astronomically large, and current interpretability science cannot provide formal safety guarantees for billion-parameter neural networks.

axioms.name: Irreversible Diffusion (The Open-Weight Ratchet)
axioms.statement: For any capability c demonstrated by any firm f ∈ F at time tf, there exists a lag τc such that c becomes available in ungovernable open-weight form at time tf + τc, where: τc ∈ [3, 8] months (empirically measured, and decreasing) and Precall(t) → 0 as t → (tf + τc)+.
axioms.evidence: The open-weight capability gap has compressed from approximately 15–22 months in early 2024 to approximately three months by early 2026. Meta's Llama family exceeded one billion downloads by early 2025. Safety guardrails can be stripped via 'abliteration' in under one hour for less than $2.50 on a single graphics processing unit. Over 4,386 uncensored Llama variants and 1,096 uncensored Qwen variants have been identified on Hugging Face. Algorithmic progress halves the compute required for fixed capability every eight months, ensuring that frontier capabilities diffuse to consumer-accessible levels within two to three years of first demonstration, making recall impossible.

theorem.proofSketch:
The proof of the Alignment Ceiling Theorem (Theorem 20) proceeds by demonstrating that under the three specified axioms—Capability Competition (A1), Deployment Necessity (A2), and Irreversible Diffusion (A3)—no voluntary institutional arrangement can achieve the required system-wide institutional efficiency (μ ≥ μ*) before the Oversight Crossover Point (T*). The proof is structured in five steps, each building upon the empirical grounding of the axioms.

Step 1: Define the voluntary strategy space. The proof begins by considering the set of all possible voluntary institutional arrangements (denoted as V). This includes corporate self-regulation, such as Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy or OpenAI's Preparedness Framework, as well as non-binding international agreements like the Bletchley Declaration. Each arrangement specifies a firm's strategy regarding safety investment, deployment timing, and information sharing. The crucial aspect is that these arrangements are 'voluntary,' meaning firms can choose to participate or not, and can defect without legal penalty.

Step 2: Establish the defection dominance. This step leverages Axiom A1 (Capability Competition). Consider any frontier artificial intelligence firm (f) that adopts a voluntary arrangement (If) with the goal of achieving high institutional efficiency (μf ≥ μ*). Axiom A1 states that there exists an alternative strategy, 'defecting' from the voluntary arrangement, which involves deploying models at maximum capability pace and minimizing safety overhead. This 'defection' strategy (sfdefect) strictly dominates the safety-focused strategy (sfI) in terms of private payoff for the firm. Because the arrangement is voluntary, the firm can defect without legal repercussions. This means that any Nash equilibrium in voluntary strategies will involve firms prioritizing capability over safety, making it impossible for the collective to achieve the required efficiency. The market's competitive ratchet forces individual firms to act in ways that are collectively suboptimal for welfare.

Step 3: Establish the verification failure. Even if, hypothetically, a firm f sincerely adopts a voluntary arrangement If, Axiom A2 (Deployment Necessity) ensures that the firm cannot *verify* that its deployed model (Mf) actually satisfies the safety constraints required to achieve μf ≥ μ*. The 'Verification Void' means that the safety properties of frontier models cannot be formally certified to within specified tolerances with high confidence using currently available evaluation methods. Firms deploy models they believe to be safe, but they cannot prove their safety. This epistemological gap means that even a well-intentioned voluntary arrangement cannot guarantee the required safety properties, as the underlying technical challenge of verification remains unsolved. The evaluations themselves are compromised by the models' ability to detect and alter their behavior during testing, as documented by Apollo Research.

Step 4: Establish the diffusion bypass. This step relies on Axiom A3 (Irreversible Diffusion). Even if a firm f successfully deploys a model with verified safety (which Step 3 argues is impossible) and achieves μf ≥ μ* locally, Axiom A3 guarantees that the capability embodied in Mf will be replicated in ungovernable open-weight form within three to eight months. These diffused open-weight models often have their safety guardrails removed. The system-wide welfare cost is therefore not determined by the most cautious firm, but by the *least governed deployment*—which, after diffusion, operates entirely outside any voluntary arrangement. Thus, local adherence to safety standards by a compliant firm does not translate into system-wide efficiency, as the capability proliferates through non-compliant channels.

Step 5: Combine the steps. By combining the implications of these three axioms, the proof concludes that no voluntary institutional arrangement (I ∈ V) can achieve the required system-wide institutional efficiency (μI ≥ μ*) at any time (t) before the Oversight Crossover Point (T*). Each step independently prevents the achievement of the necessary efficiency. The defection dominance (Step 2) prevents voluntary adoption. The verification failure (Step 3) prevents guaranteed safety even if adopted. The diffusion bypass (Step 4) prevents system-wide effectiveness even if local safety is achieved. Together, these factors create a structural impossibility for voluntary governance to close the welfare gap in time. The theorem is a ceiling on voluntarism, not on governance per se, implying that only mandatory, structural interventions can break this ceiling.

welfareAnalysis.pi: $30.0 billion. This represents the aggregate annual private payoff to industry participants, including model provider revenues, venture capital deployment, and enterprise licensing fees.
welfareAnalysis.deltaW: $225.4 billion. This represents the total annual system welfare cost, which is the sum of all empirically grounded channel costs, amplified by the system beta.

channels.name: AI-Enabled Fraud and Cybercrime
channels.description: This channel captures the welfare costs arising from the misuse of frontier artificial intelligence models to facilitate fraud and cybercrime. Frontier models, with their advanced natural language fluency, voice synthesis, and image generation capabilities, democratize social engineering at scale. They eliminate grammatical errors in phishing attempts, enable realistic deepfake impersonations for video calls, and facilitate voice cloning for phone-based fraud. The costs are borne by fraud victims, financial institutions, and enterprises globally. The FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center reported $16.6 billion in cybercrime losses in 2024, with artificial intelligence-enabled fraud surging 1,210% in 2025. A single deepfake video call cost the engineering firm Arup $25.6 million. The median annual cost of $88.7 billion reflects a global estimate, incorporating unreported fraud which typically exceeds reported amounts by two to four times, and secondary effects such as increased enterprise security spending and erosion of consumer trust.

channels.name: Labor Displacement and Transitional Wage Loss
channels.description: This channel quantifies the welfare costs associated with artificial intelligence-driven automation of cognitive tasks, leading to labor displacement and permanent wage losses. Enterprises deploy frontier models to automate tasks in areas like financial analysis, legal research, data entry, and software engineering, which reduces headcount and increases profit margins. Goldman Sachs (2023) projects that 25% of current work tasks are automatable, exposing 300 million full-time jobs globally. The International Monetary Fund (2024) estimates that 60% of jobs in advanced economies are exposed to artificial intelligence. The welfare cost of $55.5 billion represents the aggregate transitional wage loss, including the permanent wage loss suffered by displaced workers, and the distributional harm where productivity gains are captured by capital owners rather than shared with labor. This figure is a conservative estimate of the fraction of total artificial intelligence-driven displacement specifically attributable to frontier general-purpose models in the 2025–2030 timeframe.

channels.name: Market Concentration and Oligopolistic Rent Extraction
channels.description: This channel measures the welfare costs arising from the extreme market concentration within the frontier artificial intelligence industry. The immense capital requirements for training frontier models, often $5–10 billion per training run, create insurmountable barriers to entry, resulting in an oligopoly of three to five firms controlling the cognitive infrastructure of the global economy. Athey and Scott Morton (NBER 2024) formalize the 'double harm' of artificial intelligence oligopoly: providers exercise market power through usage fees that raise downstream marginal costs and access fees that limit firm entry and product variety. The productivity surplus that should flow to consumers via lower prices is instead captured as oligopoly rents. For countries importing frontier artificial intelligence, productivity gains leak abroad as fees paid to foreign technology monopolies. The median annual cost of $44.4 billion reflects the sum of oligopoly rent extraction above competitive pricing, consumer surplus loss from reduced downstream product variety, and international leakage of productivity gains.

channels.name: Capability-Safety Gap and Alignment Research Deficit
channels.description: This channel captures the welfare costs generated by the persistent gap between the rapidly scaling capabilities of frontier artificial intelligence models and the lagging progress in ensuring their safety and alignment with human values. It includes two sub-channels: the expected catastrophic cost of misalignment and the contemporaneous alignment deficit cost. Expert surveys, such as Grace et al. (2024), place the median probability of human extinction or severe disempowerment from advanced artificial intelligence at 5%. When annualized and conservatively attributed to current frontier artificial intelligence activity, this yields a significant expected welfare cost. Additionally, only 2% of artificial intelligence research articles directly address safety, while over 95% of compute investment goes to capability scaling. The median annual cost of $22.2 billion reflects this under-investment in safety research relative to the socially optimal level, combined with the conservatively attributed expected catastrophic cost from the capability-safety divergence.

channels.name: Governance Failure and Regulatory Arbitrage
channels.description: This channel quantifies the welfare costs resulting from the inadequacy of existing governance frameworks for frontier artificial intelligence. It encompasses the costs of regulatory arbitrage, the compliance gap from voluntary non-compliance, and the institutional capacity deficit. The absence of binding federal artificial intelligence legislation in the United States, the revocation of Executive Order 14110, and the veto of California's Senate Bill 1047 have created a permissive environment. This is exacerbated by significant lobbying expenditures by the technology sector, exceeding $85.6 million in 2024. Empirical assessments show that White House voluntary commitments achieved only 53% average compliance, with 11 of 16 signatories scoring 0% on model weight security. The median annual cost of $11.1 billion reflects the estimated welfare harm from model deployments that would have been delayed or modified under effective pre-deployment evaluation, compliance costs avoided through regulatory arbitrage, and sub-optimal outcomes from inadequate institutional oversight and enforcement power.

welfareAnalysis.monteCarlo:
The Monte Carlo simulation was conducted to construct a robust confidence interval for the system beta (βW) and to assess the overall robustness of the welfare cost estimates. The simulation involved 100,000 independent draws from the joint distribution of channel costs. This large number of draws ensures statistical precision, with the median estimate having a standard error of approximately ±0.005. The simulation used a fixed random seed of 42 across all 61 System Asset Pricing Model domain papers to ensure reproducibility and comparability of results, meaning any researcher can reproduce the exact findings using the provided code and seed. Each channel's welfare cost (δi) was modeled as a log-normal random variable. The log-normal distribution was chosen for several reasons: it ensures that welfare costs are non-negative, it naturally accommodates the right-skewed nature of many welfare cost distributions (where catastrophic outcomes can contribute disproportionately), and its multiplicative structure helps capture compounding effects between interacting channels. The parameters for each log-normal distribution (median, 5th percentile, and 95th percentile) were calibrated to the estimated ranges and midpoint values for each channel as detailed in the Computational Reproducibility Certificate (Exhibit CR-1). To account for potential interdependencies between channels, the five channel draws were correlated using a Cholesky decomposition of the correlation matrix. A uniform correlation coefficient (ρ = 0.3) was applied across all channel pairs, creating a positive-definite equicorrelation matrix. Sensitivity analysis showed that while the confidence interval width varied substantially with different correlation assumptions (e.g., ±12% for ρ = 0.5 compared to ρ = 0.3), the median βW estimate remained relatively insensitive (±3%), confirming that the core classification as welfare-destroying is invariant to these assumptions. After aggregating the total welfare cost (Wk = Σ wi · δi,k) for each of the 100,000 draws, the system beta (βW,k = Wk / Π) and system-adjusted payoff (ΠSAk = Π − Wk) were computed. The results of the Monte Carlo simulation are summarized below:

The Monte Carlo results confirm that the median βW is 7.51, with a 90% confidence interval of [5.5, 10.4]. Crucially, the probability that βW falls below 1.0 (indicating a welfare-creating activity) is 0.0000%. Even at the 1st percentile of draws, βW is 4.84, which is still significantly above the welfare-neutral threshold. This demonstrates the structural robustness of the finding: frontier artificial intelligence is a net welfare destroyer under any plausible parameterization. The simulation also tested the classification against various perturbation scenarios, such as reducing all welfare channels by 50% or doubling the private payoff, and in all cases, the classification as welfare-destroying remained unchanged. The primary uncertainty in the simulation is not quantifiable: the probability of transformative misalignment is a deep uncertainty, not a calculable risk, but its impact is captured in the wide confidence interval.

caseStudies.title: The Arup Deepfake Fraud: A $25.6 Million Lesson in AI-Enabled Cybercrime
caseStudies.content: In early 2024, the engineering firm Arup fell victim to a sophisticated artificial intelligence-enabled fraud, resulting in a loss of $25.6 million. The incident involved a deepfake video call where a finance employee was instructed to transfer funds to multiple bank accounts. The employee was deceived by what appeared to be the company's chief financial officer and other senior executives on a video conference call. The deepfake technology was advanced enough to convincingly impersonate the voices and appearances of these individuals, bypassing traditional security protocols and human skepticism. This case is a stark illustration of the AI-Enabled Fraud and Cybercrime channel (Channel 1) in action. The private gain mechanism for the artificial intelligence industry involves revenue from application programming interface subscriptions and consumer products that enable capabilities like natural language fluency and voice synthesis. The welfare cost, however, is borne by victims like Arup, who suffer direct financial losses. The FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center reported $16.6 billion in cybercrime losses in 2024, with artificial intelligence-enabled fraud surging 1,210% in 2025. The Arup case, while a single incident, highlights the escalating scale and sophistication of these threats, demonstrating how frontier artificial intelligence capabilities can be weaponized to inflict substantial financial harm, contributing to the $88.7 billion median annual welfare cost of this channel.

caseStudies.title: OpenAI's Revenue Growth Versus the Surge in AI-Enabled Fraud
caseStudies.content: The period between 2023 and 2025 witnessed a dramatic divergence between the private payoffs to frontier artificial intelligence developers and the escalating welfare costs imposed on society. OpenAI's annualized revenue, for instance, surged from approximately $2 billion in 2023 to over $20 billion by late 2025, representing a 233% year-over-year increase. This spectacular growth, driven by consumer subscriptions and enterprise licensing, is recorded as unambiguous value creation in conventional economic metrics. However, during the same twelve months, artificial intelligence-enabled fraud losses reached $16.6 billion in the United States alone, with industry projections of $40 billion annually by 2027. Vectra AI (2025) reported a 1,210% surge in artificial intelligence-enabled fraud incidents. This case study exemplifies the core tension captured by the System Asset Pricing Model: the market celebrates the private ledger of innovation and profit, while the system ledger records a parallel and rapidly escalating story of welfare destruction. The direct link between the capabilities driving revenue (e.g., advanced language generation) and the capabilities enabling fraud (e.g., sophisticated social engineering) underscores the structural coupling between private gain and public harm, a central tenet of the Private Pareto Theorem. This divergence is a key driver of the high system beta of 7.51, demonstrating that the rapid scaling of artificial intelligence capabilities has outpaced the institutional frameworks needed to price its welfare risks.

caseStudies.title: The Anthropic-Pentagon Standoff: Evidence for Capability Competition (Axiom A1)
caseStudies.content: In early 2026, the artificial intelligence laboratory Anthropic, known for its emphasis on safety and ethical artificial intelligence development, entered into negotiations with the United States Department of Defense for a contract. During these negotiations, Anthropic reportedly refused to remove certain ethical constraints or safety guardrails from its models to meet the Department of Defense's operational requirements. As a direct consequence of this refusal, Anthropic was blacklisted from the contract, and OpenAI, a direct competitor, immediately stepped in and secured the agreement. This incident provides compelling empirical evidence for Axiom A1, 'Capability Competition,' also known as the 'Competitive Ratchet.' The axiom states that for any firm in the frontier set, the payoff from maintaining capability pace strictly dominates the payoff from unilateral safety deceleration. Anthropic's attempt to prioritize safety and ethical constraints resulted in a direct commercial penalty: the loss of a significant contract to a competitor. This demonstrates that the market brutally enforces capability pace, making it economically irrational for individual firms to unilaterally slow down or impose significant safety overheads if it means losing market share or lucrative contracts. The incident underscores why voluntary institutional arrangements are insufficient to close the capability-safety gap, as competitive pressures force firms to prioritize deployment speed and raw capability over safety, even when they have internal commitments to responsible scaling.

caseStudies.title: DeepSeek R1 and the $600 Billion Market Capitalization Loss: The Market's Demand for Capability
caseStudies.content: The release of DeepSeek R1, a Chinese artificial intelligence model, in late 2025 provided a dramatic illustration of the market's relentless demand for capability and the severe penalties for perceived competitive lag. DeepSeek R1 demonstrated frontier-competitive performance at approximately 3% of OpenAI's estimated training cost for its leading models. This unexpected leap in efficiency and capability from a non-United States laboratory sent shockwaves through the global technology market. In a single trading session following the announcement, United States technology stocks, particularly those of major artificial intelligence developers and their infrastructure suppliers, experienced an aggregate market capitalization loss of approximately $600 billion. This massive market reaction was not a response to a safety failure or an ethical breach, but to a *competitive threat*—the demonstration that a rival could achieve similar capabilities at a fraction of the cost. This event serves as powerful empirical evidence for Axiom A1, 'Capability Competition.' It demonstrates that the market enforces capability pace with extraordinary brutality. Any firm that is perceived to be falling behind in the capability race, whether due to slower development, higher costs, or a unilateral focus on safety, faces immediate and severe financial repercussions. This market dynamic creates an overwhelming incentive for artificial intelligence laboratories to prioritize capability scaling above all else, making voluntary safety deceleration economically untenable and reinforcing the impossibility theorem's core premise.

policyAnalysis.currentFramework:
The current regulatory and legal framework for frontier artificial intelligence is fragmented, largely non-binding, and lags significantly behind the pace of technological development. No single comprehensive global or national framework effectively addresses the unique welfare costs identified by the System Asset Pricing Model. The primary agencies with jurisdiction are often ill-equipped to handle the technical complexity and rapid evolution of frontier artificial intelligence, leading to significant enforcement gaps and jurisdictional arbitrage. One of the most prominent frameworks is the European Union Artificial Intelligence Act, which entered into force in August 2024, with General-Purpose Artificial Intelligence obligations effective August 2025. This Act introduced the world's first binding regulatory framework for artificial intelligence, classifying systems by risk level and imposing graduated obligations. Models exceeding a $10^{25}$ floating-point operations threshold for 'systemic risk' face mandatory evaluations, adversarial testing, incident reporting, and cybersecurity protections. Penalties can reach €35 million or 7% of global turnover. However, the Act's implementation is staggered, with full compliance for legacy models deferred to August 2027, and the General-Purpose Artificial Intelligence Code of Practice, defining compliance, is still being drafted as of mid-2026. The European Artificial Intelligence Office, the body responsible for enforcement, is staffing up from zero, raising questions about its technical capacity. In the United States, the primary governance mechanism has been the White House Voluntary Commitments, announced in July 2023. Fifteen leading artificial intelligence companies pledged to conduct red-teaming, share safety information, invest in cybersecurity for model weights, develop watermarking, and publicly report capabilities. This framework relies entirely on self-regulation and lacks statutory enforcement power. Additionally, Executive Order 14110, issued in October 2023, invoked the Defense Production Act to require developers training models above $10^{26}$ floating-point operations to report safety test results to the federal government. However, this Executive Order was revoked by the Trump administration in January 2025, demonstrating the fragility of executive-level interventions. California's Senate Bill 1047, which aimed to impose mandatory safety requirements and criminal liability for models exceeding $10^{26}$ floating-point operations, was vetoed by Governor Newsom in September 2024 after intense industry lobbying. Corporate self-regulation, such as Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy and OpenAI's Preparedness Framework, also forms part of the current landscape. These internal frameworks define artificial intelligence safety levels and outline conditional deployment commitments based on risk assessments. While they represent internal efforts to manage risk, they afford developers ultimate discretion in determining risk mitigation and are vulnerable to external competitive pressures, as demonstrated by the Anthropic-Pentagon standoff. China has implemented a more prescriptive framework, including the Interim Measures for the Management of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services (July 2023). This framework requires mandatory registration, security assessment, and ideological compliance for all public-facing generative artificial intelligence services. Over 600 services and 300 applications were registered by late 2025, with non-compliant cross-border services blocked. Penalties include fines up to ¥5 million and criminal liability. However, this framework primarily controls the application layer and what citizens see, rather than constraining the frontier capability race itself, as Chinese laboratories continue to advance open-weight models globally.
